topics.topic: The Nature of Science and Scientific Exploration
topics.meaning: Science is a way of exploring and understanding the world by observing, questioning, experimenting and reasoning.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is not just a collection of facts; it is a way of thinking and exploring the world around us. Scientists observe things, ask questions, make guesses (hypotheses), test them through experiments, and draw conclusions. This careful, step-by-step process is called the scientific method. Curiosity and keen observation are the starting points of science. By exploring the natural world in this way, we keep discovering new things and finding answers to our questions, which makes science an exciting and never-ending journey.
topics.easyExplanation: Science is a way of exploring the world: observe, ask questions, guess, test and conclude. Curiosity is where it all begins.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Science is exploration
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Science is a way of exploring and understanding the world, not just facts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe and question
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Scientists observe carefully and ask questions about what they see.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test ideas
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: They make guesses (hypotheses) and test them through experiments.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Draw conclusions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the results they draw conclusions and discover new things.

examples: Wondering why ice melts and testing it is a scientific exploration.
Observing that plants bend towards light and asking why is doing science.

importantKeywords.definition: A way of exploring and understanding the natural world.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Studying why the sky is blue is science.

importantKeywords.definition: Carefully noticing things using our senses.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Noticing that ice floats is an observation.

importantKeywords.definition: A guess that can be tested.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Guessing a plant grew taller due to sunlight is a hypothesis.

topics.meaning: Scientific knowledge is ever-evolving, as new evidence and ideas improve or change what we know.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is described as 'ever-evolving' because our understanding keeps growing and changing. As scientists make new observations and experiments, they gather new evidence that can support, improve or even replace older ideas. Many things we know today were discovered by building on the work of earlier scientists, and our knowledge will keep improving in the future. This shows that science is open to change and correction, and that being curious, questioning and willing to learn are very important. Scientists across the world also work together to advance knowledge.
topics.easyExplanation: Science keeps evolving: new evidence improves or changes old ideas. Scientists build on each other's work, and our knowledge keeps growing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Knowledge grows
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Our scientific understanding keeps growing and changing over time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: New evidence
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: New observations and experiments give evidence that can change older ideas.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Building on others
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Scientists build on the work of earlier scientists.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Open to change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Science is open to correction, so questioning and learning are important.

examples: Ideas about the solar system improved as better telescopes were invented.
New evidence can replace an old scientific idea with a better one.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Experiments give us evidence.

importantKeywords.definition: To develop and change gradually.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Scientific ideas evolve over time.

importantKeywords.definition: Finding out something new.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A discovery can change what we know.

revisionNotes: Science is ever-evolving.

chapters.chapter: Exploring Substances: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral

topics.meaning: Substances can be acidic, basic or neutral depending on their properties; acids taste sour and bases taste bitter.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many substances around us can be grouped as acidic, basic or neutral. Acidic substances usually taste sour, like lemon juice, vinegar and tamarind. Basic substances usually taste bitter and feel soapy, like baking soda solution and soap. Neutral substances are neither acidic nor basic, like pure water and common salt solution. We should never taste unknown substances to test them, as many are harmful; instead we use indicators. Knowing whether a substance is acidic, basic or neutral helps us understand how it behaves and use it safely.
topics.easyExplanation: Substances are acidic (sour, like lemon), basic (bitter and soapy, like soap), or neutral (like water). We never taste unknown substances to test them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three groups
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substances can be acidic, basic or neutral.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Acids
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acidic substances taste sour, like lemon juice, vinegar and tamarind.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Bases
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Basic substances taste bitter and feel soapy, like baking soda and soap.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Neutral and safety
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Neutral substances like water are neither; never taste unknown substances, use indicators instead.

examples: Lemon juice and vinegar are acidic substances.
Baking soda solution and soap are basic substances.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Lemon juice contains an acid.

importantKeywords.definition: A substance that is bitter, soapy and turns red litmus blue.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Soap is a base.

importantKeywords.definition: Neither acidic nor basic.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Pure water is neutral.

topics.meaning: Indicators are substances that change colour to show whether a substance is acidic or basic; many come from nature.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An indicator is a substance that tells us whether something is acidic or basic by changing its colour. The most common is litmus, which turns red in acids and blue in bases. Many indicators come from nature: turmeric stays yellow in acids but turns red in bases, and the China rose (red rose) extract turns acids pink/magenta and bases green. We use indicators by adding a drop of the substance or dipping indicator paper and watching the colour change. Indicators are a safe and easy way to test substances without tasting them.
topics.easyExplanation: Indicators change colour to show acids and bases. Litmus turns red in acid, blue in base; turmeric turns red in bases; rose extract changes colour too.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An indicator changes colour to show if a substance is acidic or basic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Turmeric

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: China rose extract turns acids pink and bases green.

topics.meaning: Neutralisation is the reaction between an acid and a base that produces a neutral substance, releasing heat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an acid and a base are mixed in the right amounts, they react and cancel out each other's effects, forming a neutral substance (salt and water). This reaction is called neutralisation, and it usually releases heat. Neutralisation is useful in daily life. For example, when we have acidity (too much acid) in the stomach, we take a mild base called an antacid to neutralise it; farmers add lime (a base) to neutralise acidic soil; and a basic substance is used to soothe an ant or bee sting which is acidic. Neutralisation helps us deal with harmful acids and bases safely.
topics.easyExplanation: When an acid and a base mix, they cancel each other out, forming a neutral substance. This neutralisation is used in antacids, treating soil and soothing stings.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A basic substance soothes an acidic ant or bee sting.

topics.meaning: An electric cell is a source of electricity, and two or more cells joined together make a battery.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric cell is a device that stores chemical energy and changes it into electrical energy to make things work, like in a torch or a toy. A cell has two terminals, a positive (+) and a negative (-) terminal. When we join two or more cells together in the correct way (positive of one to negative of the next), they form a battery, which gives more power than a single cell. Cells and batteries are the common sources of electricity for many small devices we use every day.
topics.easyExplanation: An electric cell gives electricity and has a + and a - terminal. Joining two or more cells makes a battery, which gives more power.

topics.meaning: An electric circuit is a complete path through which electricity flows from a cell to a device and back.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An electric circuit is a closed path along which electricity can flow. A simple circuit has a cell (the source), connecting wires, and a device such as a bulb. Electricity flows only when the path is complete and unbroken, forming a closed circuit; then the bulb glows. If there is a break anywhere, it becomes an open circuit and no electricity flows, so the bulb does not glow. The electric current flows from the positive terminal of the cell, through the wires and device, back to the negative terminal.
topics.easyExplanation: An electric circuit is a complete path for electricity. The bulb glows only when the circuit is closed (complete). A break makes it open and the bulb goes off.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Open circuit

examples: A bulb glows when connected to a cell with wires in a complete circuit.

topics.meaning: A circuit diagram shows a circuit using symbols, and a switch is used to turn a circuit on or off.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Instead of drawing the actual components, we represent an electric circuit using simple symbols in a circuit diagram, which is quick and clear. Each component, like a cell, bulb, wire and switch, has its own symbol. A switch is a device that opens or closes a circuit to turn it off or on. When the switch is 'on', it closes the circuit and current flows; when it is 'off', it opens the circuit and current stops. Switches let us control electrical devices easily, like turning a light on and off.
topics.easyExplanation: A circuit diagram shows a circuit using simple symbols. A switch turns a circuit on (closed) or off (open), letting us control devices.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A circuit diagram represents a circuit using simple symbols.

topics.meaning: Conductors allow electricity to pass through them, while insulators do not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Materials can be grouped by whether they let electricity pass through. Materials that allow electricity to flow through them are called electrical conductors, such as most metals like copper, aluminium and iron. Materials that do not allow electricity to flow through them are called insulators, such as plastic, rubber, wood and glass. We test a material by placing it in a circuit and seeing if the bulb glows. Conductors are used to make wires, while insulators are used to cover wires and make switch handles, keeping us safe from electric shocks.
topics.easyExplanation: Conductors (like metals) let electricity pass; insulators (like plastic, rubber, wood) do not. Wires are conductors covered with insulators for safety.

chapters.chapter: The World of Metals and Non-metals

topics.meaning: Metals and non-metals are two groups of materials with different physical properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Materials can be classified into metals and non-metals based on their properties. Metals are usually hard, shiny (have lustre), can be beaten into thin sheets (malleable), can be drawn into wires (ductile), make a ringing sound when struck (sonorous), and conduct heat and electricity. Examples are iron, copper, aluminium and gold. Non-metals are usually not shiny (dull), are often soft or brittle, do not make a ringing sound, and do not conduct heat and electricity well. Examples are sulphur, carbon and oxygen. Knowing these properties helps us choose the right material for different uses.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals are shiny, hard, malleable, ductile, sonorous and conduct heat and electricity (like iron, copper). Non-metals are dull, brittle and poor conductors (like sulphur).

topics.topic: Reactions of Metals with Air and Water (Rusting)
topics.meaning: Many metals react with air and water; iron, for example, rusts when exposed to moist air.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metals can react with air and water, and one important example is the rusting of iron. When iron is exposed to both air (oxygen) and moisture (water) for some time, a reddish-brown layer called rust forms on its surface. Rust slowly damages and weakens the iron object. Rusting can be prevented by keeping iron away from air and moisture, for example by painting it, applying oil or grease, or coating it with another metal (galvanising). Some metals react with air or water more easily than others, and these reactions are important to understand for using and protecting metals.
topics.easyExplanation: Iron rusts when exposed to both air and moisture, forming a reddish-brown layer that weakens it. Painting or oiling iron prevents rusting.

topics.meaning: Metals and non-metals are used in many ways in daily life based on their properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Both metals and non-metals are very useful because of their special properties. Metals are used to make tools, machines, vehicles, utensils, wires and jewellery, because they are strong, can be shaped and conduct electricity. Non-metals are also essential to life and industry: oxygen is needed for breathing, carbon is found in all living things, and non-metals are used in fertilisers, medicines and many products. So both metals and non-metals play important roles. Choosing whether to use a metal or non-metal depends on the properties needed for the job.

chapters.chapter: Changes Around Us: Physical and Chemical

topics.meaning: A physical change is a change in which no new substance is formed and which is often reversible.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many changes happen around us all the time. In a physical change, the substance may change its shape, size or state, but no new substance is formed, and the change can often be reversed. For example, melting of ice into water, tearing of paper, dissolving sugar in water, and stretching a rubber band are physical changes. In each case the substance is still the same material; ice and water are both water. Physical changes are usually easy to reverse, such as freezing water back into ice.
topics.easyExplanation: In a physical change, no new substance forms and it can often be reversed, like ice melting into water or tearing paper. The material stays the same.

topics.meaning: A chemical change is a change in which one or more new substances are formed, and it is usually irreversible.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a chemical change, one or more new substances with new properties are formed, and the change is usually difficult to reverse. Signs of a chemical change include the formation of a new substance, change in colour, giving off a gas, change in smell, or release or absorption of heat and light. Examples are the burning of paper or wood, rusting of iron, cooking of food, and souring of milk. In each, the original substance changes into something new. Chemical changes are very important in nature and in our daily lives.
topics.easyExplanation: In a chemical change, a new substance forms and it is usually hard to reverse, like burning paper, rusting iron or cooking food. Signs include new colour, gas or heat.

topics.meaning: Changes that can be undone are reversible, while changes that cannot easily be undone are irreversible.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Changes can also be grouped by whether they can be undone. A reversible change is one that can be changed back to the original, such as melting and freezing of water, or folding and unfolding paper. An irreversible change cannot easily be changed back to the original, such as burning a candle, cooking an egg or ripening of fruit. Many physical changes are reversible, while most chemical changes are irreversible because a new substance is formed. Understanding this helps us know whether we can get the original substance back.

topics.meaning: Nature shows many slow and fast changes, some desirable and some not, that shape the world around us.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Changes happen continuously in nature, some fast and some very slow. Fast changes happen quickly, like the bursting of a cracker or the burning of paper, while slow changes take a long time, like the rusting of iron, the ripening of fruit, the weathering of rocks or the formation of soil. Some changes are desirable (useful), like cooking food or ripening of fruit, while others are undesirable (harmful), like the spoiling of food or rusting of iron. Recognising and understanding these natural changes helps us make use of the helpful ones and prevent the harmful ones.

chapters.chapter: Adolescence: A Stage of Growth and Change

topics.topic: Growing With Age: The Teenage Years
topics.meaning: Adolescence is the stage of life, usually from about 10 to 19 years, when the body grows quickly and changes from a child to an adult.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Adolescence is an important stage of life, usually between about 10 and 19 years of age, when a child gradually grows into an adult. During these teenage years, the body grows rapidly in height and weight. There is a sudden increase in height because the bones grow longer. This growth happens at different rates for different individuals, and girls often start a little earlier than boys. Adolescence is a normal and healthy part of growing up, and the changes are caused by special chemicals in the body called hormones.
topics.easyExplanation: Adolescence (about 10 to 19 years) is when a child grows into an adult. The body grows fast, especially in height, due to chemicals called hormones.

topics.meaning: During adolescence, the body undergoes changes that show it is maturing and developing reproductive ability.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: During adolescence, the body develops in ways that show it is maturing into an adult capable of reproduction. Both boys and girls go through changes controlled by hormones. These include changes in body shape, growth of hair in new areas, changes in the voice (especially deeper in boys), and the development of reproductive organs. Girls and boys experience some different changes. All these changes are a normal and natural part of growing up, and they happen gradually. It is important to understand them so that adolescents feel comfortable and take care of their bodies.

topics.meaning: Adolescents experience emotional and behavioural changes, including new feelings and mood changes, as they grow up.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Along with physical changes, adolescents also go through emotional and behavioural changes. They may feel more independent, have new and stronger emotions, experience mood swings, and become more self-conscious about their appearance. They may also want more privacy and may form stronger friendships. These changes are caused partly by hormones and partly by growing up and facing new situations. It is normal to feel confused or moody at times. Talking to parents, teachers or trusted elders, and treating others with respect and kindness, helps adolescents handle these changes in a healthy way.

topics.meaning: During adolescence, good nutrition, personal hygiene, physical activity and a balanced lifestyle are very important.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Because the body changes and grows rapidly during adolescence, it is very important to take good care of health. This includes eating a balanced diet rich in proteins, vitamins and minerals for proper growth, and avoiding junk food. Personal hygiene is essential, like bathing regularly and keeping the body clean, since adolescents sweat more. Regular physical activity and exercise keep the body fit and the mind fresh. Enough sleep and a balanced social life with good company are also important. Avoiding harmful habits and maintaining cleanliness help adolescents stay healthy in body and mind.

topics.meaning: Conduction is the transfer of heat through a solid from its hotter part to its colder part, without the material itself moving.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Heat always flows from a hotter object to a colder one. Conduction is the way heat travels through solids. When one end of a solid object is heated, the heat passes from the hot end to the cold end through the material, while the material itself does not move from its place. For example, if one end of a metal spoon is placed in hot tea, the other end also becomes warm. Materials that allow heat to pass easily are good conductors (most metals), while those that do not are poor conductors or insulators (wood, plastic).

topics.meaning: Convection is the transfer of heat in liquids and gases by the actual movement of the heated material.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Convection is the way heat travels through liquids and gases (fluids). When a fluid is heated, the hot part becomes lighter and rises up, while the cooler, heavier part sinks down to take its place. This sets up a continuous movement called a convection current, which carries heat throughout the fluid. For example, water in a pan heats up by convection, and warm air rises while cool air sinks. Convection also causes natural events like land and sea breezes, where air moves between the land and the sea because they heat and cool at different rates.

topics.meaning: Radiation is the transfer of heat without any material medium, and it is how heat reaches us from the Sun.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Radiation is the third way heat travels, and it does not need any material to pass through. Heat travels as invisible rays and can pass even through empty space (vacuum). This is how heat from the Sun reaches the Earth across empty space, and how we feel the warmth of a fire even without touching it. Dark and dull surfaces absorb more heat radiation, while light and shiny surfaces reflect it. Heat transfer drives many natural processes, such as the water cycle (where the Sun's heat evaporates water) and the weathering of rocks. The three ways heat travels, conduction, convection and radiation, together shape weather and nature.

topics.meaning: Time is measured using events that repeat regularly, and the SI unit of time is the second.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To measure time, we use events that repeat at regular intervals. Long ago, people used natural repeating events like the rising and setting of the Sun, the phases of the Moon, and devices like sundials and water clocks. A simple pendulum, which swings back and forth in a regular way, was an important discovery for measuring time; the time it takes for one complete swing is called its time period, and it is always the same for a given pendulum. The SI (standard) unit of time is the second (s); larger units are the minute (60 s) and the hour (60 min). Modern clocks measure time very accurately.

topics.meaning: Speed is how fast an object moves, found by dividing the distance travelled by the time taken.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Speed tells us how fast or slow an object is moving. It is defined as the distance travelled by an object in a given time. We calculate speed using the formula: speed = distance / time. The unit of speed depends on the units of distance and time, for example metres per second (m/s) or kilometres per hour (km/h). An object that covers more distance in the same time has a greater speed. By knowing any two of speed, distance and time, we can find the third. Speed helps us compare how fast different objects, like a walking person and a moving car, are travelling.

topics.meaning: Motion is uniform when an object covers equal distances in equal times, and non-uniform when it does not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an object moves, its motion can be uniform or non-uniform. In uniform motion, the object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time, so its speed stays the same, like a car moving steadily at 40 km/h. In non-uniform motion, the object covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time, so its speed keeps changing, like a bus that speeds up and slows down in traffic. We can show motion using a distance-time graph: a straight line shows uniform motion, while a curved line shows non-uniform motion. Understanding these helps us describe how objects move.

topics.meaning: Nutrition is the process by which animals take in food and break it down through digestion to get energy and materials.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Animals cannot make their own food, so they take it in from outside; this is called nutrition. The food must be broken down into simpler substances that the body can absorb and use; this breaking down is called digestion. In human beings, digestion takes place in the digestive system, which includes the mouth, food pipe, stomach and intestines. Food is chewed in the mouth, churned in the stomach, and digested further in the intestines, where useful substances are absorbed into the blood, and the undigested waste is removed. Different animals digest food in different ways suited to what they eat.

topics.meaning: Respiration is the process by which animals take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide to get energy from food.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Respiration is the life process by which living things release energy from food. Animals take in oxygen from the air and use it to break down food inside their cells, releasing energy and giving out carbon dioxide. The taking in of oxygen and giving out of carbon dioxide is called breathing, which is part of respiration. Different animals breathe through different organs: humans and many animals use lungs, fish use gills to breathe in water, insects use tiny tubes, and earthworms breathe through their moist skin. Respiration provides the energy that animals need to live and carry out all their activities.

topics.meaning: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their own food using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Unlike animals, green plants make their own food through a process called photosynthesis. This takes place mainly in the green leaves, which contain a green substance called chlorophyll. In photosynthesis, the leaves take in carbon dioxide from the air through tiny pores called stomata, absorb water from the soil through the roots, and use the energy of sunlight captured by chlorophyll to make food (a sugar) and release oxygen. Because plants make food, they are called producers, and almost all other living things depend on them. Photosynthesis also keeps the air balanced by using carbon dioxide and giving out oxygen.
topics.easyExplanation: Green plants make their own food by photosynthesis, using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide in their leaves (with chlorophyll), and release oxygen.

topics.meaning: Plants transport water, minerals and food to all their parts through special tube-like tissues.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Just as our body carries materials around, plants also transport substances to all their parts. Water and minerals are absorbed from the soil by the roots and carried upwards to the leaves through tube-like tissues called xylem. The food made in the leaves by photosynthesis is carried to all other parts of the plant, like the stem, roots, flowers and fruits, through another set of tubes called phloem. This transport system makes sure every part of the plant gets the water, minerals and food it needs to grow and stay alive.

topics.meaning: Plants also respire, taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide to get energy from their food.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Like all living things, plants also respire to get energy from their food. During respiration, plant cells take in oxygen and use it to break down food, releasing energy and giving out carbon dioxide. Respiration happens all the time, day and night, in all parts of the plant, including the leaves, stems and roots. Gases are exchanged through tiny pores in the leaves (stomata) and through openings in the stems and roots. It is important to note that photosynthesis (making food) happens only in light, while respiration (using food for energy) happens all the time.
topics.easyExplanation: Plants respire too, taking in oxygen and giving out carbon dioxide to get energy. Respiration happens day and night, unlike photosynthesis which needs light.

topics.topic: Sources of Light and Straight-line Travel
topics.meaning: Light comes from luminous sources and travels in a straight line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We are able to see things because of light. Objects that give out their own light are called luminous objects, such as the Sun, a lighted bulb and a flame. Objects that do not give out their own light but are seen because they reflect light are called non-luminous objects, like the Moon, a book or a chair. An important property of light is that it travels in a straight line. We can show this because light cannot bend around corners, and it is the reason that shadows form and that we cannot see around bends. This straight-line travel of light explains many things we see every day.
topics.easyExplanation: Light comes from luminous objects (Sun, bulb) and travels in a straight line. Non-luminous objects (Moon, book) are seen by reflected light.

topics.meaning: Objects are classified by how much light passes through them as transparent, translucent or opaque.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Different objects let different amounts of light pass through them. Transparent objects allow almost all light to pass through, so we can see clearly through them, like clear glass and water. Translucent objects allow only some light to pass through, so we can see only blurred images through them, like frosted glass or oily paper. Opaque objects do not allow any light to pass through, so we cannot see through them at all, like wood, metal and a book. Opaque objects block light and form clear shadows, while transparent objects barely form shadows.

topics.meaning: A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light travelling in a straight line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A shadow is the dark area that forms when an opaque object comes in the path of light. Because light travels in a straight line, the opaque object blocks it and a dark shape is formed on the other side, on a screen or the ground. To form a shadow we need a source of light, an opaque object, and a screen to catch the shadow. A shadow is always of the same shape as the outline of the object, and it is black regardless of the colour of the object. The size of the shadow changes with the position of the light and the object; for example, the Sun makes long shadows in the morning and evening and short shadows at noon.

topics.topic: Reflection and Images in a Plane Mirror
topics.meaning: Light bounces off shiny surfaces (reflection), and a plane mirror forms a clear image of objects.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When light falls on a shiny, smooth surface like a mirror, it bounces back; this bouncing back of light is called reflection. A plane mirror is a flat, shiny surface that reflects light to form a clear image. The image formed in a plane mirror has special features: it is the same size as the object, it is upright (the right way up), it appears to be as far behind the mirror as the object is in front, and it is laterally inverted, meaning left and right are exchanged (your right hand looks like the left hand of the image). A pinhole camera, which uses a tiny hole instead of a mirror, forms a small, inverted image, showing that light travels in straight lines.

topics.topic: Rotation of the Earth (Day and Night)
topics.meaning: The Earth spins on its axis, which causes day and night.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Earth spins, or rotates, on its own imaginary axis like a top, taking about 24 hours to complete one rotation. Because of this rotation, at any moment one half of the Earth faces the Sun and has daytime, while the other half faces away and has night-time. As the Earth keeps turning, places move from day into night and from night into day. This is why the Sun appears to rise in the east and set in the west, even though it is really the Earth that is moving. The rotation of the Earth gives us the regular cycle of day and night.

topics.topic: Revolution of the Earth and Seasons
topics.meaning: The Earth revolves around the Sun once a year, and its tilt causes the seasons.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides spinning on its axis, the Earth also moves around the Sun in a fixed path called its orbit; this movement is called revolution, and it takes about 365 days (one year) to complete. The Earth's axis is tilted, and because of this tilt, different parts of the Earth receive different amounts of sunlight at different times of the year. This is what causes the seasons, summer, winter and others, as the Earth revolves around the Sun. When a part of the Earth is tilted towards the Sun it has summer, and when tilted away it has winter. So the revolution of the tilted Earth around the Sun gives us the changing seasons through the year.

topics.meaning: The Moon appears to change its shape over a month because we see different amounts of its sunlit part.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Moon does not give out its own light; we see it because it reflects sunlight. The Moon moves around the Earth, taking about a month to complete one round. As it moves, we see different amounts of the Moon's sunlit half from the Earth, so the Moon appears to change its shape from night to night. These different shapes are called the phases of the Moon. They range from the thin crescent, to the half Moon, to the bright full Moon, and back to the new Moon (when we cannot see the Moon at all). The phases repeat in a regular cycle of about a month.

topics.meaning: An eclipse happens when one heavenly body comes in the shadow of another; there are solar and lunar eclipses.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An eclipse occurs when the Sun, the Earth and the Moon come in a straight line, so that one casts a shadow on the other. In a solar eclipse, the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth and blocks the Sun's light, so the Sun is hidden, fully or partly, for people in the Moon's shadow; this happens on a new Moon day. In a lunar eclipse, the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon, so the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon and the Moon is hidden; this happens on a full Moon day. We should never look directly at a solar eclipse, as it can harm our eyes. Eclipses are natural events caused by the straight-line travel of light and the movement of these bodies.
topics.easyExplanation: An eclipse happens when the Sun, Earth and Moon line up. In a solar eclipse the Moon hides the Sun; in a lunar eclipse the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon.
